title: Granulomatous inflammation
body_html:
Background
Granulomatous Inflammation is a form of chronic inflammation that arises when a stimulus cannot be eradicated by the acute inflammatory response.

It is characterised histologically by a collection of epithelioid macrophages (≥5), often with surrounding lymphocytes and sometimes fibroblasts. Epithelioid macrophages are activated/altered macrophages specialised for killing and phagocytosis; they may fuse to form multinucleated giant cells.

Pathogenesis

Initial trigger:
  • Stimuli resistant to digestion/clearance (e.g. organisms with digestion-resistant capsules like Mycobacterium tuberculosis, or inert materials such as sutures, silica, glass).
Early response:
  • A transient acute inflammatory response occurs.
Failure of clearance:
  • If clearance ineffective, a chronic granulomatous response develops.
Mediators:
  • IFN-γ: key initiator that activates macrophages, their transformation into epithelioid cells and ultimately granuloma formation. IFN-γ is absolutely essential for granuloma formation.
  • IL-1: contributes to the inflammatory milieu, recruits immune cells, assisting in the initial formation of granulomas.
  • IL-2: enlarges granulomas.
  • TNF-α: maintains granulomas.
  • Overall mechanism: Type IV hypersensitivity reaction, mediated by Th1 CD4+ T cells.

Causes
All share the feature of being resistant to resolution by acute inflammation.

CategoryExamples
Infectious
  • Mycobacterium tuberculosis (caseating granulomas)
  • Leprosy
  • Parasitic diseases
  • Fungal infections (e.g. Histoplasma, Cryptococcus)
Foreign bodies
  • Sutures
  • Silica
  • Glass
  • Talc
Autoimmune
  • Rheumatoid nodules
Malignant
  • Lymphoma and some other cancers
Others
  • Sarcoidosis
  • Drug reactions
  • Crohn’s disease
  • Berylliosis

Types:
  • Caseating granulomas: central necrosis and 'cheese-like' e.g. TB, fungal infection.
  • Non-caseating granulomas: no necrosis e.g. sarcoidosis, Crohn’s, foreign body.

Clinical relevance
Granulomas contain difficult-to-eradicate stimuli but may impair tissue/organ function (e.g. pulmonary fibrosis in TB or sarcoidosis). The Histological type (caseating vs non-caseating) provides diagnostic clues.

Key points summary
Granulomatous inflammation is a Type IV hypersensitivity-mediated chronic inflammation, arising when acute responses fail to clear resistant stimuli (infectious, foreign body, autoimmune, malignant, drugs, or others), driven by IL-1, IL-2, TNF-α, IFN-γ and characterised by epithelioid macrophages forming granulomas.
